title: Sarga 6
summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: Themes: devotion. The passage moves from “Tormented by them, the gods and the sages whose wealth was penance, distressed by fear, went to Maheśvara, the god of gods, for refuge.” through “Thus, Mali, Sumali, and Malyavan, along with their battle-eager leaders, torment us; therefore, O god, you ought to grant protection to us who…” through “Then, indeed, those afflicted by the fear of night-wanderers (Rakshasas) acclaimed Lord Shiva with cries of victory and approached Lord Vishnu.”

Themes: devotion, virtue and right conduct. The passage moves from “Having bowed down and greatly honored the god bearing the conch and discus, they spoke agitatedly concerning the sons of Sukesha.” through “Thus addressed by the gods, Janardana, the God of gods, who grants fearlessness to his devotees and fear to his enemies, indeed spoke to the…” through “It is said that the gods and sages, having met Shankara, spoke these words, desiring our destruction.”

Themes: devotion, mortality and immortality. The passage moves from “O god, the sons of Sukesha, who are exceedingly arrogant due to the power of boons and possess terrible forms, harass us at every step.” through “Having obtained the mantra from Hara and saluted Shiva, they reached the abode of Narayana and submitted everything to him.” through “All these Asuras and Danavas, though endowed with great strength and power, are never heard to have remained undefeated once they entered battle.”

Themes: devotion, mind and meditation, virtue and right conduct. The passage moves from “All of them had performed hundreds of sacrifices, were skilled in various illusions, proficient in all weapons, and struck terror into their…” through “O lord of Rakshasas, there is indeed no inherent reason for the god Vishnu to act against us; it is only due to the fault of the other gods that…” through “They also advanced with crocodiles, tortoises, fish, birds resembling Garuḍa, lions, tigers, boars, antelopes, and yaks.”

Themes: devotion, mortality and immortality. The passage moves from “All the Rākṣasas, proud of their strength and enemies of the gods, abandoned Lanka and set out for the world of the gods to fight.” through “There, jackals, frightful to behold and making terrible, thunder-like sounds and loud roars, were howling.” through “All those night-wandering demons indeed took refuge in Malyavan, just as gods resort to Brahma, and as one would take refuge in an unmoving…”

Themes: devotion, mind and meditation, freedom from attachment. The passage moves from “That army of the demon lords, resounding like the crashing of great clouds, went to the world of gods, which was under Mali's sway, with a…” through “The Lord, having mounted the mountain-like Garuda, the son of Vinata, swiftly departed for the destruction of the Rakshasas.” through “Then, the night-wandering Rakshasas, surrounding Madhava, pierced him by thousands with their excellent weapons, which were smeared with blood…”

verses.sanskrit: तैर्वध्यमाना देवाश्च ऋषयश्च तपोधनाः । भयार्ताः शरणं जग्मुर्देवदेवं महेश्वरम् ।। 7.6.1 ।।
verses.translation: Tormented by them, the gods and the sages whose wealth was penance, distressed by fear, went to Maheśvara, the god of gods, for refuge.

verses.sanskrit: जगत्सृष्ट्यन्तकर्तारमजमव्यक्तरूपिणम् । आधारं सर्वलोकानामाराध्यं परमं गुरुम् ।। 7.6.2 ।।
verses.translation: They went to him who is the creator and destroyer of the world, unborn, of unmanifest form, the support of all worlds, adorable, and the supreme teacher.

verses.sanskrit: ते समेत्य तु कामारिं त्रिपुरारिं त्रिलोचनम् । ऊचुः प्राञ्जलयो देवा भयगद्गदभाषिणः ।। 7.6.3 ।।
verses.translation: Having approached Shiva, the enemy of Kama, the destroyer of Tripura, and the three-eyed one, the gods, with folded hands and voices choked by fear, spoke to him.

verses.sanskrit: सुकेशपुत्रैर्भगवन्पितामहवरोद्धतैः । प्रजाध्यक्ष प्रजाः सर्वा बाध्यन्ते रिपुबाधनैः ।। 7.6.4 ।।
verses.translation: The gods addressed Shiva, saying, "O Lord, O Lord of creatures, all beings are being tormented by the sons of Sukesha, who are uplifted by the boon of Brahma and act as tormentors."

verses.sanskrit: शरण्यान्यशरण्यानि ह्याश्रमाणि कृतानि नः । स्वर्गाच्च देवान्प्रच्याव्य स्वर्गे क्रीडन्ति देववत् ।। 7.6.5 ।।
verses.translation: They have made our hermitages unsafe, and having driven the gods from heaven, they now play in heaven as if they were gods themselves.

verses.sanskrit: अहं विष्णुरहं रुद्रो ब्रह्माहं देवराडहम् । अहं यमश्च वरुणश्चन्द्रो ऽहं रविरप्यहम् ।। 7.6.6 ।।
verses.translation: They boast, "I am Vishnu, I am Rudra, I am Brahma, I am Indra, I am Yama, and Varuna, I am Chandra, and I am also Ravi."

verses.sanskrit: इति माली सुमाली च माल्यवांश्चैव राक्षसाः । बाधन्ते समरोद्धर्षा ये च तेषां पुरःसराः । तन्नो देव भयार्तानामभयं दातुमर्हसि ।। 7.6.7 ।।
verses.translation: Thus, Mali, Sumali, and Malyavan, along with their battle-eager leaders, torment us; therefore, O god, you ought to grant protection to us who are afflicted by fear.

verses.sanskrit: अशिवं वपुरास्थाय जहि वै देवकण्टकान् ।। 7.6.8 ।।
verses.translation: Therefore, assuming a terrible form, indeed destroy these thorns of the gods.

verses.sanskrit: धन्ते समरोद्धर्षा ये च तेषां पुरःसराः । इत्युक्तस्तु सुरैः सर्वैः कपर्दी नीललोहितः । सुकेशं प्रति सापेक्षः प्राह देवगणान्प्रभुः ।। 7.6.9 ।।
verses.translation: After all the gods had thus spoken about those battle-eager leaders who torment them, Lord Shiva, with matted hair and blue-red form, concerned about Sukesha, spoke to the hosts of gods.

verses.sanskrit: अहं तान्न हनिष्यामि मयावध्या हि ते सुराः । किं तु मन्त्रं प्रदास्यामि यो वै तान्निहनिष्यति ।। 7.6.10 ।।
verses.translation: Lord Shiva declared that he would not kill those powerful beings (Rakshasas) because they were invulnerable to him, but he would provide a strategy that would indeed destroy them.

verses.sanskrit: एतमेव समुद्योगं पुरस्कृत्य महर्षयः । गच्छध्वं शरणं विष्णुं हनिष्यति स तान्प्रभुः ।। 7.6.11 ।।
verses.translation: O great sages, taking this very plan as your guide, go seek refuge in Lord Vishnu, for that Lord will surely kill them.

verses.sanskrit: ततस्तु जयशब्देन प्रतिनन्द्य महेश्वरम् । विष्णोः समीपमाजग्मुर्निशाचरभयार्दिताः ।। 7.6.12 ।।
verses.translation: Then, indeed, those afflicted by the fear of night-wanderers (Rakshasas) acclaimed Lord Shiva with cries of victory and approached Lord Vishnu.

verses.sanskrit: शङ्खचक्रधरं देवं प्रणम्य बहुमान्य च । ऊचुः सम्भ्रान्तवद्वाक्यं सुकेशतनयान्प्रति ।। 7.6.13 ।।
verses.translation: Having bowed down and greatly honored the god bearing the conch and discus, they spoke agitatedly concerning the sons of Sukesha.

verses.sanskrit: सुकेशतनयैर्देव त्रिभिस्त्रेताग्निसन्निभैः । आक्रम्य वरदानेन स्थानान्यपहृतानि नः ।। 7.6.14 ।।
verses.translation: O Lord, our abodes have been seized and taken away by the three sons of Sukesha, who resemble the three sacred fires, by virtue of a boon.

verses.sanskrit: लङ्का नाम पुरी दुर्गा त्रिकूटशिखरे स्थिता । तत्र स्थिताः प्रबाधन्ते सर्वान्नः क्षणदाचराः ।। 7.6.15 ।।
verses.translation: There is an impregnable city named Lanka, situated on the peak of Trikuta mountain, where night-roaming Rakshasas reside and torment all of us.

verses.sanskrit: स त्वमस्मद्धितार्थाय जहि तान्मधुसूदन । शरणं त्वां वयं प्राप्ता गतिर्भव सुरेश्वर ।। 7.6.16 ।।
verses.translation: O Madhusudana, for our welfare, you must destroy them; we have sought refuge in you, so be our protector, O Lord of gods.

verses.sanskrit: चक्रकृत्तास्यकमलान्निवेदय यमाय वै । भयेष्वभयदो ऽस्माकं नान्यो ऽस्ति भवता विना ।। 7.6.17 ।।
verses.translation: Offer to Yama those whose lotus-like faces are severed by your discus, for there is no other giver of fearlessness to us in times of fear except you.

verses.sanskrit: राक्षसान्समरे दुष्टान्सानुबन्धान्मदोद्धतान् । नुदं त्वं नो भयं देव नीहारमिव भास्करः ।। 7.6.18 ।।
verses.translation: O God, just as the sun dispels mist, you should dispel our fear by destroying these wicked, arrogant Rakshasas and their followers in battle.

verses.sanskrit: इत्येवं दैवतैरुक्तो देवदेवो जनार्दनः । अभयं भयदो ऽरीणां दत्त्वा देवानुवाच ह ।। 7.6.19 ।।
verses.translation: Thus addressed by the gods, Janardana, the God of gods, who grants fearlessness to his devotees and fear to his enemies, indeed spoke to the gods after granting them protection.

verses.sanskrit: सुकेशं राक्षसं जाने ईशानवरदर्पितम् । तांश्चास्य तनयाञ्जाने येषां ज्येष्ठः स माल्यवान् ।। 7.6.20 ।।
verses.translation: I know the demon Sukesha, who is arrogant due to a boon from Shiva, and I also know his sons, among whom Malyavan is the eldest.

verses.sanskrit: तानहं समतिक्रान्तमर्यादान्राक्षसाधमान् । निहनिष्यामि सङ्क्रुद्धः सुरा भवत विज्वराः ।। 7.6.21 ।।
verses.translation: I, being exceedingly angry, will kill those vile demons who have transgressed all limits; therefore, O gods, be free from fear.

verses.sanskrit: इत्युक्तास्ते सुराः सर्वे विष्णुना प्रभविष्णुना । यथावासं ययुर्हृष्टाः प्रशंसन्तो जनार्दनम् ।। 7.6.22 ।।
verses.translation: Thus addressed by the all-powerful Vishnu, all the gods, delighted, returned to their respective abodes, praising Janardana.

verses.sanskrit: विबुधानां समुद्योगं माल्यवांस्तु निशाचरः । श्रुत्वा तौ भ्रातरौ वीराविदं वचनमब्रवीत् ।। 7.6.23 ।।
verses.translation: Malyavan, the demon, having heard of the gods' concerted effort, spoke these words to his two brave brothers.

verses.sanskrit: अमरा ऋषयश्चैव सङ्गम्य किल शकरम् । अस्मद्वधं परीप्सन्त इदं वचनमब्रुवन् ।। 7.6.24 ।।
verses.translation: It is said that the gods and sages, having met Shankara, spoke these words, desiring our destruction.

verses.sanskrit: सुकेशतनया देव वरदानबलोद्धताः । बाधन्ते ऽस्मान्समुद्दृप्ता घोररूपाः पदे पदे ।। 7.6.25 ।।
verses.translation: O god, the sons of Sukesha, who are exceedingly arrogant due to the power of boons and possess terrible forms, harass us at every step.

verses.sanskrit: राक्षसैरभिभूताः स्म न शक्ताः स्म प्रजापते । स्वेषु सद्मसु संस्थातुं भयात्तेषां दुरात्मनाम् ।। 7.6.26 ।।
verses.translation: O Prajapati, we are overcome by these Rakshasas and are unable to remain in our own abodes due to fear of those wicked beings.

verses.sanskrit: तदस्माकं हितार्थाय जहि तांश्च त्रिलोचन । राक्षसान्हुङ्कृतेनैव दह प्रदहतां वर ।। 7.6.27 ।।
verses.translation: Therefore, O three-eyed one, for our welfare, destroy those Rakshasas and burn them merely with a roar, O best among those who can burn.

verses.sanskrit: इत्येवं त्रिदशैरुक्तो निशम्यान्धकसूदनः । शिरः करं च धुन्वान इदं वचनमब्रवीत् ।। 7.6.28 ।।
verses.translation: Having thus been addressed by the gods and having heard their plea, the destroyer of Andhaka (Shiva), shaking his head and hand, spoke these words.

verses.sanskrit: अवध्या मम ते देवाः सुकेशतनया रणे । मन्त्रं तु वः प्रदास्यामि यस्तान्वै निहनिष्यति ।। 7.6.29 ।।
verses.translation: O gods, those sons of Sukesha are not to be killed by me in battle; however, I shall give you a counsel (or a plan) that will indeed lead to their destruction.

verses.sanskrit: यो ऽसौ चक्रगदापाणिः पीतवासा जनार्दनः । हरिर्नारायणः श्रीमान् शरणं तं प्रपद्यथ ।। 7.6.30 ।।
verses.translation: You should take refuge in that glorious Narayana, also known as Janardana and Hari, who holds the discus and mace in his hands and wears yellow garments.

verses.sanskrit: हरादवाप्य ते मन्त्रं कामारिमभिवाद्य च । नारायणालयं प्राप्य तस्मै सर्वं न्यवेदयन् ।। 7.6.31 ।।
verses.translation: Having obtained the mantra from Hara and saluted Shiva, they reached the abode of Narayana and submitted everything to him.

verses.sanskrit: ततो नारायणेनोक्ता देवा इन्द्रपुरोगमाः । सुरारींस्तान्हनिष्यामि सुरा भवत विज्वराः ।। 7.6.32 ।।
verses.translation: Then Narayana addressed the gods, led by Indra, saying, "I will kill those enemies of the gods; O gods, become free from anxiety."

verses.sanskrit: देवानां भयभीतानां हरिणा राक्षसर्षभौ । प्रतिज्ञातो वधो ऽस्माकं चिन्त्यतां यदिह क्षमम् ।। 7.6.33 ।।
verses.translation: O best of Rakshasas, Hari has promised the destruction of us (Rakshasas) to the terrified gods; therefore, let us consider what is appropriate to do in this situation.

verses.sanskrit: हिरण्यकशिपोर्मृत्युरन्येषां च सुरद्विषाम् । नमुचिः कालनेमिश्च संह्रादो वीरसत्तमः ।। 7.6.34 ।।
verses.translation: The death of Hiranyakashipu and other enemies of the gods, such as Namuchi, Kalanemi, and Samhrada, the best of heroes, was also caused by him.

verses.sanskrit: राधेयो बहुमायी च लोकपालो ऽथ धार्मिकः । यमलार्जुनौ च हार्दिक्यः शुम्भश्चैव निशुम्भकः ।। 7.6.35 ।।
verses.translation: This verse lists several powerful and deceitful Asuras, including Radheya, Bahumayi, Lokapala, Dharmika, Yamala and Arjuna, Hardikya, Shumbha, and Nishumbhaka.

verses.sanskrit: असुरा दानवाश्चैव सत्त्ववन्तो महाबलाः । सर्वे समरमासाद्य न श्रूयन्ते ऽपराजिताः ।। 7.6.36 ।।
verses.translation: All these Asuras and Danavas, though endowed with great strength and power, are never heard to have remained undefeated once they entered battle.

verses.sanskrit: सर्वैः क्रतुशतैरिष्टं सर्वे मायाविदस्तथा । सर्वे सर्वास्त्रकुशलाः सर्वे शत्रुभयङ्कराः ।। 7.6.37 ।।
verses.translation: All of them had performed hundreds of sacrifices, were skilled in various illusions, proficient in all weapons, and struck terror into their enemies.

verses.sanskrit: नारायणेन निहताः शतशो ऽथ सहस्रशः । एतज्ज्ञात्वा तु सर्वेषां क्षमं कर्तुमिहार्हथ ।। 7.6.38 ।।
verses.translation: Knowing that these very beings were slain by Narayana in hundreds and thousands, you should now grant forgiveness to all of them here.

verses.sanskrit: ततः सुमाली माली च श्रुत्वा माल्यवतो वचः । ऊचतुर्भ्रातरं ज्येष्ठं भगांशाविव वासवम् ।। 7.6.39 ।।
verses.translation: Then, Sumali and Mali, having heard the words of Malyavan, spoke to their elder brother, just as Bhaga and Amsha would speak to Indra.

verses.sanskrit: स्वधीतं दत्तमिष्टं चाप्यैश्वर्यं परिपालितम् । आयुर्निरामयं प्राप्तं सुधर्मः प्रापितः पथि ।। 7.6.40 ।।
verses.translation: We have diligently studied, given charity, performed sacrifices, maintained our sovereignty, attained a healthy life, and established righteousness on our path.

verses.sanskrit: देवसागरमक्षोभ्यं शस्त्रैः समवगाह्य च । जिता द्विषो ह्यप्रतिमास्तन्नो मृत्युकृतं भयम् ।। 7.6.41 ।।
verses.translation: Having plunged into the unagitable ocean of gods with our weapons, and having conquered unequalled enemies, we therefore have no fear caused by death.

verses.sanskrit: नारायणश्च रुद्रश्च शक्रश्चापि यमस्तथा । अस्माकं प्रमुखे स्थातुं सर्वे बिभ्यति सर्वदा ।। 7.6.42 ।।
verses.translation: Narayana, Rudra, Shakra, and Yama, all of them always fear to stand in our presence.

verses.sanskrit: विष्णोर्देवस्य नास्त्येव कारणं राक्षसेश्वर । देवानामेव दोषेण विष्णोः प्रचलितं मनः ।। 7.6.43 ।।
verses.translation: O lord of Rakshasas, there is indeed no inherent reason for the god Vishnu to act against us; it is only due to the fault of the other gods that Vishnu's mind has been agitated.

verses.sanskrit: तस्मादद्य समुद्युक्ताः सर्वसैन्यसमावृताः । देवानेव जिघांसाम एभ्यो दोषः समुत्थितः ।। 7.6.44 ।।
verses.translation: Therefore, today, fully prepared and surrounded by all our armies, we shall kill the gods, for the fault has originated from them.

verses.sanskrit: एवं सम्मन्त्र्य बलिनः सर्वे सैन्यसमावृताः । उद्योगं घोषयित्वा तु सर्वे नैर्ऋतपुङ्गवाः ।। युद्धाय निर्ययुः क्रुद्धा जम्भवृत्रबला इव ।। 7.6.45 ।।
verses.translation: Having thus deliberated, all the powerful and foremost Rākṣasas, surrounded by their armies, announced their preparations and set out for battle, enraged like the demons Jambha, Vṛtra, and Bala.

verses.sanskrit: इति ते राम सम्मन्त्र्य सर्वोद्योगेन राक्षसाः । युद्धाय निर्ययुः सर्वे महाकाया महाबलाः ।। 7.6.46 ।।
verses.translation: O Rama, having thus deliberated with full preparation, all those Rākṣasas of huge bodies and great strength set out for battle.

verses.sanskrit: स्यन्दनैर्वारणैश्चैव हयैश्च गिरिसन्निभैः । खरैर्गोभी रथोष्ट्रैश्च शिंशुमारैर्भुजङ्गमैः ।। 7.6.47 ।।
verses.translation: They set out with chariots, elephants, mountain-like horses, donkeys, cows, camel-drawn chariots, porpoises, and serpents.

verses.sanskrit: मकरैः कच्छपैर्मीनैर्विहङ्गैर्गरुडोपमैः । सिंहैर्व्याघ्रैर्वराहैश्च सृमरैश्चमरैरपि ।। 7.6.48 ।।
verses.translation: They also advanced with crocodiles, tortoises, fish, birds resembling Garuḍa, lions, tigers, boars, antelopes, and yaks.

verses.sanskrit: त्यक्त्वा लङ्कां गताः सर्वे राक्षसा बलगर्विताः । प्रयाता देवलोकाय योद्धुं दैवतशत्रवः ।। 7.6.49 ।।
verses.translation: All the Rākṣasas, proud of their strength and enemies of the gods, abandoned Lanka and set out for the world of the gods to fight.

verses.sanskrit: लङ्काविपर्ययं दृष्ट्वा यानि लङ्कालयान्यथ । भूतानि भयदर्शीनि विमनस्कानि सर्वशः ।। 7.6.50 ।।
verses.translation: Seeing the impending destruction of Lanka, all the beings dwelling there became utterly fearful and disheartened.

verses.sanskrit: रथोत्तमैरुह्यमानाः शतशो ऽथ सहस्रशः ।। 7.6.51 ।।
verses.translation: Hundreds and thousands of them were being carried by excellent chariots.

verses.sanskrit: प्रयाता राक्षसास्तूर्णं देवलोकं प्रयत्नतः । रक्षसामेव मार्गेण दैवतान्यपचक्रमुः ।। 7.6.52 ।।
verses.translation: The Rakshasas quickly departed to the world of gods with great effort, and even the deities departed by the very path used by the Rakshasas.

verses.sanskrit: भौमाश्चैवान्तरिक्षाश्च कालाज्ञप्ता भयावहाः । उत्पाता राक्षसेन्द्राणामभावाय समुत्थिताः ।। 7.6.53 ।।
verses.translation: Terrifying earthly and atmospheric portents, commanded by fate, arose for the destruction of the chief Rakshasas.

verses.sanskrit: अस्थीनि मेघा ववृषुरुष्णं शोणितमेव च । वेलां समुद्राश्चोत्क्रान्ताश्चेलुश्चाप्यथ भूधराः ।। 7.6.54 ।।
verses.translation: Clouds rained bones and hot blood, oceans overflowed their shores, and mountains also trembled.

verses.sanskrit: अट्टहासान्विमुञ्चन्तो घननादसमस्वनाः । वाश्यन्त्यश्च शिवास्तत्र दारुणं घोरदर्शनाः ।। 7.6.55 ।।
verses.translation: There, jackals, frightful to behold and making terrible, thunder-like sounds and loud roars, were howling.

verses.sanskrit: सम्पतन्त्यथ भूतानि दृश्यन्ते च यथाक्रमम् । गृध्रचक्रं महच्चात्र ज्वलनोद्गारिभिर्मुखैः ।। 7.6.56 ।।
verses.translation: Then, ominous spirits were seen appearing successively, and a large flock of vultures with flame-emitting mouths was also present.

verses.sanskrit: राक्षसानामुपरि खे भ्रमते ऽलातचक्रवत् । कपोता रक्तपादाश्च शारिका विद्रुता ययुः ।। काका वाश्यन्ति तत्रैव बिडाला वै द्विपादयः ।। 7.6.57 ।।
verses.translation: Above the Rakshasas, the vultures hovered in the sky like a fiery wheel, while red-footed pigeons and mynah birds fled, and two-footed cats and crows cawed right there.

verses.sanskrit: उत्पातांस्ताननादृत्य राक्षसा बलगर्विताः । यान्त्येव न निवर्त्तन्ते मृत्युपाशावपाशिताः ।। 7.6.58 ।।
verses.translation: Disregarding those omens, the Rakshasas, proud of their strength and bound by the noose of death, continued to advance and did not turn back.

verses.sanskrit: माल्यवांश्च सुमाली च माली च सुमहाबलाः । आसन्पुरःसरास्तेषां क्रतूनामिव पावकाः ।। 7.6.59 ।।
verses.translation: Malyavan, Sumali, and Mali, all exceedingly mighty, were the leaders of those Rakshasas, just as fires are foremost in sacrifices.

verses.sanskrit: माल्यवन्तं च ते सर्वे माल्यवन्तमिवाचलम् । निशाचरा ह्याश्रयन्ति धातारमिव देवताः ।। 7.6.60 ।।
verses.translation: All those night-wandering demons indeed took refuge in Malyavan, just as gods resort to Brahma, and as one would take refuge in an unmoving mountain.

verses.sanskrit: तद्बलं राक्षसेन्द्राणां महाभ्रघननादितम् । जयेप्सया देवलोकं ययौ मालिवशे स्थितम् ।। 7.6.61 ।।
verses.translation: That army of the demon lords, resounding like the crashing of great clouds, went to the world of gods, which was under Mali's sway, with a desire for victory.

verses.sanskrit: राक्षसानां समुद्योगं तं तु नारायणः प्रभुः । देवदूतादुपश्रुत्य चक्रे युद्धे तदा मनः ।। 7.6.62 ।।
verses.translation: But the Lord Nārāyaṇa, having heard of that preparation by the Rākṣasas from a messenger of the gods, then set his mind on battle.

verses.sanskrit: स सज्जायुधतूणीरो वैनतेयोपरि स्थितः । आसज्ज्य कवचं दिव्यं सहस्रार्कसमद्युति ।। 7.6.63 ।।
verses.translation: He, with weapons and quivers ready, stood upon Vainateya (Garuda), having donned his divine armor that shone with the brilliance of a thousand suns.

verses.sanskrit: आबध्य शरसम्पूर्णे इषुधी विमले तदा । श्रोणिसूत्रं च खड्गं च विमलं कमलेक्षणः ।। शङ्खचक्रगदाशार्ङ्गखड्गाख्यप्रवरायुधान् ।। 7.6.64 ।।
verses.translation: Then, the lotus-eyed Nārāyaṇa fastened his two pure quivers full of arrows, his pure waist-band, and his sword, along with his excellent weapons: the conch, discus, mace, Śārṅga bow, and sword.

verses.sanskrit: सुपर्णं गिरिसङ्काशं वैनतेयमथास्थितः । राक्षसानामभावाय ययौ तूर्णतरं प्रभुः ।। 7.6.65 ।।
verses.translation: The Lord, having mounted the mountain-like Garuda, the son of Vinata, swiftly departed for the destruction of the Rakshasas.

verses.sanskrit: सुपर्णपृष्ठे स बभौ श्यामः पीताम्बरो हरिः । काञ्चनस्य गिरेः शृङ्गे सतडित्तोयदो यथा ।। 7.6.66 ।।
verses.translation: Dark-complexioned Hari, adorned in yellow garments, shone on Garuda's back like a lightning-streaked cloud upon the peak of a golden mountain.

verses.sanskrit: स सिद्धदेवर्षिमहोरगैश्च गन्धर्वयक्षैरुपगीयमानः । समाससादासुरसैन्यशत्रूंश्चक्रासिशार्ङ्गायुधशङ्खपाणिः ।। 7.6.67 ।।
verses.translation: Praised by Siddhas, Devas, Rishis, great serpents, Gandharvas, and Yakshas, he, holding the discus, sword, Sharnga bow, and conch, approached the enemies of the Asura army.

verses.sanskrit: सुपर्णपक्षानिलनुन्नपक्षं भ्रमत्पताकं प्रविकीर्णशस्त्रम् । चचाल तद्राक्षसराजसैन्यं चलोपलं नील इवाचलेन्द्रः ।। 7.6.68 ।।
verses.translation: That army of the Rakshasa king, with its banners scattered by the wind from Garuda's wings, fluttering flags, and scattered weapons, trembled like a blue mountain king with dislodged rocks.

verses.sanskrit: ततः शरैः शोणितमांसरूषितैर्युगान्तवैश्वानरतुल्यविग्रहैः । निशाचराः सम्परिवार्य माधवं वरायुधैर्निर्बिभिदुः सहस्रशः ।। 7.6.69 ।।
verses.translation: Then, the night-wandering Rakshasas, surrounding Madhava, pierced him by thousands with their excellent weapons, which were smeared with blood and flesh and resembled the destructive fire at the end of an eon. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये श्रीमदुत्तरकाण्डे षष्ठः सर्गः ।। 6 ।। Thus ends the sixth sarga of Uttara Kanda of the holy Ramayana the first epic composed by sage Valmiki.
